Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed nominal-prepositional phrase: noun + preposition-like particle + dative singular noun; functions adverbially as a predicate complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Something is held dearly, remembered with affection, or treated as emotionally central.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on βεκος, the famous Phrygian word for bread, extended metaphorically from daily nourishment to emotional sustenance in Anatolian household speech.
First Example Sentence: ταν ματεραν βεκος εν καρδιαι εχω.
Second Example Sentence: οροαν πατηρ βεκος εν καρδιαι λεγει.
Third Example Sentence: ευκιν αγαθην βεκος εν καρδιαι εσταται.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative infinitival proverb: object noun + directional phrase + negated infinitive; used as a complete sentential maxim.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not invite danger or trouble into a safe place.
Usage Contexts: warnings, household advice, politics, risk management
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the Anatolian prestige image of the lion as force and kingship; the doorway evokes home, tomb, and civic boundary in Phrygian inscriptions.
First Example Sentence: μη τον λιον εις θυραν ουκ αγειν, εφη ο γερον.
Second Example Sentence: εν αγοραι φιλος λιον εις θυραν ουκ αγειν μανθανει.
Third Example Sentence: οταν ερις αυξανη, λιον εις θυραν ουκ αγειν χρη.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verbal idiom with divine genitive: genitive proper noun + accusative object + infinitive verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To attempt something sacred, risky, or beyond ordinary human authority.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Refers to Μητρα, the Phrygian mother-goddess later associated with Cybele; moving her stone suggests disturbing sacred power.
First Example Sentence: βας μητρας λιθον κινειν ουκ ετολμησεν.
Second Example Sentence: ο νεος ανηρ μητρας λιθον κινειν ηθελεν.
Third Example Sentence: εν βουλαι ου παντες μητρας λιθον κινειν δυνανται.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive verbal phrase: genitive singular noun + accusative object + finite verb; predicate of temperament or event.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be loud, ecstatic, disorderly, or full of feverish excitement.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on the Corybantes, ecstatic attendants of the mother-goddess whose ritual noise and dance were famous in Anatolia and the Greek world.
First Example Sentence: η αγορα σημερον κορυβαντος κροτον εχει.
Second Example Sentence: παιδες εν οικω κορυβαντος κροτον ειχον.
Third Example Sentence: οταν οι αυλοι ηχωσι, παν το χωριον κορυβαντος κροτον εχει.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival metaphor: genitive place-name/noun + accusative abstract object + infinitive verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To chase an illusion, rumor, or impossible gain.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses αργου as an Anatolian-sounding place or territory marker; the shadow image fits rural daylight travel and marketplace exaggeration.
First Example Sentence: ου σοφος εστιν αργου σκιαν διωκειν.
Second Example Sentence: εμπορος ψευδη ακουσας αργου σκιαν διωκει.
Third Example Sentence: εν οδω μακραι αργου σκιαν διωκειν κοπον φερει.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative clause: finite past plural verb + comparative particle + nominative plural noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: They gave generously, behaved nobly, or acted with ancestral dignity.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on attested-looking Phrygian forms such as δακαρεν and πατερης; evokes lineage, gift-giving, and public remembrance in inscriptions.
First Example Sentence: εν εορται οι ανδρες δακαρεν ως πατερες.
Second Example Sentence: τοις ξενοις δακαρεν ως πατερες και χαριν ελαβον.
Third Example Sentence: ο δημος μικρος ην, αλλα δακαρεν ως πατερες.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated infinitival construction: accusative noun + conjunction + dative noun + infinitive verb; expresses practical alignment.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To connect promise with obligation; to make words match duties.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Echoes inscriptional legal and dedicatory language, where gift, vow, and duty often appear as socially binding acts.
First Example Sentence: γραμματευς ουεβαν κε δουμε ζευγνυναι κελευει.
Second Example Sentence: χωρις μαρτυρων ουεβαν κε δουμε ζευγνυναι χαλεπον.
Third Example Sentence: ορχος αγαθος ουεβαν κε δουμε ζευγνυναι δεικνυσι.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Demonstrative object phrase + adverb + infinitive verb; productive metaphor for elevation or exaggeration.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To exaggerate a matter, elevate a small issue, or make something seem grander than it is.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses attested-looking sequence ταν ειξα υψοδαν from Phrygian-style inscriptional material; height becomes a metaphor for inflated importance.
First Example Sentence: ρητωρ ταν ειξα υψοδαν ποιει και τον δημον πειθει.
Second Example Sentence: μη ταν ειξα υψοδαν ποιει εν μικραι εριδι.
Third Example Sentence: η μητηρ εγνω οτι παις ταν ειξα υψοδαν ποιει.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Genitive metaphor with accusative object and infinitive/finite verb; paradoxical image used as a character description.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To conceal intense anger, power, or danger under outward calm.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Combines storm imagery from Anatolian highland weather with the social value of controlled public speech before elders or assemblies.
First Example Sentence: ο βασιλευς κεραυνου σιγην φυλασσει προ της μαχης.
Second Example Sentence: η γυνη κεραυνου σιγην φυλασσει, αλλα παντες φοβουνται.
Third Example Sentence: εν συνεδριω σοφος κεραυνου σιγην φυλασσει.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical fixed clause: finite/past verbal form + accusative object + genitive noun; used as a judgment formula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person has spoken with full authority, courage, or public consequence.
Usage Contexts: oratory, legal testimony, epic narration, public oath
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on inscriptional-style forms such as ενσταρνα and αναρ; the phrase frames speech as a completed public deed rather than mere sound.
First Example Sentence: εν βουλαι ο πρεσβυς ενσταρνα φωνην αναρος.
Second Example Sentence: μαρτυς αληθης ενσταρνα φωνην αναρος ουκ εκρυψεν.
Third Example Sentence: ποιητης περι πολεως ενσταρνα φωνην αναρος εγραψεν.
